text: In this video, we present the case of a seventy-three-year-old woman who presented to our institution with signs and symptoms of acute small bowel obstruction. She has an extensive past medical history, including schizophrenia, for which she lives in a group home.

CT imaging reveals dilated small bowel loops and air within the gallbladder, highlighted by a circle on the scout image. Axial images show large calcified gallstones in the gallbladder and periduodenal inflammation with a fistulous tract from the gallbladder to the duodenum. In the pelvis, a gallstone is visible within the small intestine, causing obstruction. These findings are confirmed on coronal reconstructions.
We suspected a cholecystoduodenal fistula as well as a small bowel obstruction from a gallstone within the intestinal tract consistent with gallstone ileus.

Traditionally, gallstone ileus has two treatment goals. The first and most important being removal of the obstructing gallstone within the small intestine. In select patients, an attempt at cholecystectomy with cholecystoduodenal fistula division may be attempted. However, as most patients present moribund, the additional time and added risk may not be warranted.

In an effort to utilize the benefits of minimally invasive surgery, these patients may be dealt with in a laparoscopic manner. We present a novel option that, in the setting of patients who have large residual stones within the gallbladder lumen, A laparoscopic cholecystotomy with extraction of these stones and gallbladder closure may be utilized as a safe alternative for cholecystectomy.
The surgeon elevates the greater omentum to reveal the small bowel, clearly identifying the area of obstruction caused by the gallstone.

The surgeon places sutures and performs a longitudinal enterotomy on the small bowel to retrieve the obstructing gallstone. The enterotomy is then closed transversely to prevent stricturing of the bowel.
The surgeon examines the remaining length of the small intestine to ensure there are no additional large stones present.

The surgical team shifts focus to the right upper quadrant, where they encounter an extensive inflammatory response around the gallbladder. They carefully dissect the surrounding omentum to expose the fundus of the gallbladder.
The surgeon performs a cholecystotomy, opening the gallbladder lumen and retrieving gallstones from within.
The surgeon reveals the cholecystoduodenal fistula, with no residual stones visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon irrigates the right upper quadrant and closes the gallbladder using several interrupted figure-of-eight silk sutures.
The surgeon irrigates the upper abdomen and places a drain near the gallbladder.

To our knowledge, this is the first report of performing a cholecystotomy with stone retrieval and gallbladder closure in the setting of gallstone ileus. We believe this may be a viable option in select patients as it allows for minimally invasive techniques and avoids the added risk of bile duct injury or duodenal fistula. There is also theoretical risk of decreasing the possibility of recurrent small bowel obstructions, although this warrants further investigation.
